settings.title: What We Do Before We Ever Meet You
settings.excerpt: A look at the unglamorous prep work that makes a first strategy session worth your time.
settings.body:
People sometimes assume the value of a consultant shows up in the room. Most of ours happens in the two weeks before.

Before a kickoff, we read every public thing about your business, talk to two or three of your customers, and pull whatever numbers you are willing to share. By the time we sit down, we already have a draft of where we think the friction is.

What the prep actually involves

The reading is not glamorous. We go through your website, your reviews, your social posts, and anything a competitor has said about your corner of the market. Patterns turn up quickly when you look at all of it at once, and they are usually patterns the owner has stopped noticing.

The customer calls matter even more. A few honest conversations with people who buy from you tell us more than a stack of internal reports. We ask what nearly stopped them from buying and what almost sent them elsewhere, because that is where the real work tends to be.

By the time you see us, our prep has usually covered four things.

  • Everything public about your business and your market
  • Short calls with two or three of your customers
  • Whatever figures you are comfortable sharing
  • A first draft of where we think the friction sits

Why we work this way

That preparation means the session is not spent getting up to speed. It is spent pressure-testing ideas and making decisions. We would rather you pay us to think than to listen.

If a first meeting ever feels like we are learning the basics in front of you, we have done the job poorly. The aim is for you to walk in and find that we already understand the shape of the problem, so the time together goes straight to what to do about it.
